coredata.dc:title: The effect of deposition processing on structural and luminescent properties of a-SiOC:H thin films fabricated by RF-magnetron sputtering
coredata.prism:issueName: E-MRS 2016, Symposium M: Silicon compatible materials and integrated devices for photonics and optical sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Vasin, A.V.
Rusavsky, A.V.
Kysil, D.V.
Prucnal, S.
Piryatinsky, Yu.P.
Starik, S.P.
Nasieka, Iu.
Strelchuk, V.V.
Lysenko, V.S.
Nazarov, A.N.
coredata.dc:description: Amorphous silicon oxicarbide thin films (a-SiOC(:H)) were deposited by RF-magnetron sputtering using Si or SiC target in Ar/CH4/O2 flow. Interatomic bonding and light emission properties were analyzed by FTIR in transmission and attenuation total reflection (ATR) mode, Raman scattering and photoluminescence spectroscopy. Comparison of the results obtained by FTIR and ATR allows us to suggest that structural properties of the near-surface region and the bulk of the films are significantly different. The surface layer contains a significant fraction of polymer-like SiOC structural components in the form of Si–O–Si/Si–O–C chains and cages, while bulk of the film is represented by inorganic amorphous SiOx network with amorphous carbon precipitates. Samples with the high fraction of carbon precipitates exhibit white luminescence at room temperature. The origin of strong photoluminescence is discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amorphous silicon oxicarbide thin films
FTIR
ATR
Photoluminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR transmission spectra of a-SiOC:H films deposited using Si target: series with (a) – low carbon incorporation (samples K248 and K249) and (b) high carbon incorporation (samples K244 and K245).
FTIR ATR spectra of a-SiOC:H films deposited using Si target: series with (a) – low carbon incorporation (samples K248 and K249) and (b) high carbon incorporation (samples K244 and K245).
FTIR ATR spectra of a-SiOC:H films deposited using SiC target: series with (a) – low carbon incorporation (samples K266 and K267) and (b) high carbon incorporation (samples K264 and K265).
Room temperature PL emission and excitation spectra of “carbon-rich” a-SiOC:H films deposited using (a) Si and (b) SiC targets.
Typical PL decay curves of emission normalized on intensity of excited laser pulse at 500nm, 600nm, 700nm excited by 405nm. IRF – instrument-response function.
Typical carbon-related Raman scattering spectrum of carbon-rich a-SiOC:H film.
Sample labels and corresponding deposition conditions.
A sheet of IR-bands and their relative intensity estimated inform the ATR spectra.

head.ce:title: The effect of deposition processing on structural and luminescent properties of a-SiOC:H thin films fabricated by RF-magnetron sputtering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amorphous silicon oxicarbide thin films (a-SiOC(:H)) were deposited by RF-magnetron sputtering using Si or SiC target in Ar/CH4/O2 flow. Interatomic bonding and light emission properties were analyzed by FTIR in transmission and attenuation total reflection (ATR) mode, Raman scattering and photoluminescence spectroscopy. Comparison of the results obtained by FTIR and ATR allows us to suggest that structural properties of the near-surface region and the bulk of the films are significantly different. The surface layer contains a significant fraction of polymer-like SiOC structural components in the form of Si–O–Si/Si–O–C chains and cages, while bulk of the film is represented by inorganic amorphous SiOx network with amorphous carbon precipitates. Samples with the high fraction of carbon precipitates exhibit white luminescence at room temperature. The origin of strong photoluminescence is discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Amorphous hydrogenated silicon oxicarbide thin films (a-SiOC:H) is an organic-inorganic hybrid-type material used as low-k interlayer dielectric in 3D-integrated electronic devices [1]. Visible luminescence is another challenging property of nanostructured a-SiOC(:H) materials, because realization of an efficient Si-based light source is still of high motivation of the scientific community currently working in the field of photonics. During the last decade it was widely reported that in some cases a-SiOC(:H) amorphous structural systems demonstrate a strong visible photoluminescence (PL) at room temperature. In the literature, several promising results on nanopowders [2–4], nanowires [5,6] and thin films [7,8] have been reported. However, the nature of the photoluminescence in SiOC is still a matter of discussion.

In general, the PL emission from SiOC films can originate from multiple contributions, which have been attributed to different centers, including Si–C bonds [9,10], C- and O-related defects [11–13], defects at the SiC/SiO2 interface [14] and neutral oxygen vacancies [14], while in some studies the PL was assigned to the precipitation of sp2 C clusters [4]. The present research demonstrates that carbon incorporation and precipitation is a key issue in formation of light emission centers in a-SiOC:H thin films deposited by RF-magnetron sputtering technique. Additionally, in most cases the formation technique of SiOC nanostructures or thin films demands high temperature processes [3–8]. In this paper we propose low temperature technology which can be used even for plastic materials.

The thin films used in this work were deposited by reactive RF-magnetron sputtering of Si or SiC target in flow of Ar/CH4/O2. Deposition conditions and labeling of the samples are summarized in Table 1. Thin films were deposited on the silicon wafers at 200°C. Some samples were deposited on glass (microscope slides). Methane-to-oxygen ratio and discharge power were variable deposition parameters. Bonding structure of the films was characterized by FTIR spectroscopy in common transmission mode and in attenuated total reflection (ATR) mode (Nicolet 6700). The photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) signals were recorded with a monochromator (Jobin Yvon Triax 320) and a photomultiplier (Hamamatsu H7732-10). During PL and PLE measurements samples were excited using Xe lamp radiation dispersed by a double-grating monochromator. Kinetic properties of PL relaxation were studied by time resolved spectroscopy (LIFESPEC II) using 405nm excitation. Micro-Raman measurements were carried out at room temperature in backscattering configuration using a triple Raman spectrometer T-64000 Horiba Jobin-Yvon, equipped with electrically cooled CCD detector. Ar laser radiation with wavelength of 488nm was used for excitation.

FTIR transmission spectra of a-SiOC:H films deposited using Si target are illustrated in Fig. 1. Spectra of the films deposited at low methane flow rate are quite typical for amorphous silicas and are composed of the dominant band at 1070cm−1 with a high frequency shoulder at 1200cm−1 (asymmetrical stretching TO and LO modes of Si–O–Si, respectively), and a weaker band at 800cm−1 (symmetrical stretching TO) [15]. A weak but well defined shoulder at 740cm−1 in the spectrum of the sample deposited at higher discharge power, is possibly associated with Si–C bonds (see Fig. 1(a), spectrum 2).

The increase of methane/oxygen flow rate results in strong development of C–H (2800–3000cm−1) and O–H (O–H stretching at 3000–3700cm−1, and Si–OH stretching at 960cm−1) related bands (Fig. 1(b)) indicating development of morphological porosity and precipitation of amorphous carbon in this series. Relative contribution of the high frequency shoulder of the dominant Si–O band 1000–1200cm−1 increases strongly in “carbon-rich” samples that can be assigned to carbon induced coordination disorder in SiOx structural network. Transmission FTIR spectra of the films deposited using SiC target are quite similar (not presented here).

Unfortunately, a high level of “noise”-background due to atmospheric moisture does not allow analyzing IR-bands in 1300–2000cm−1 spectral range. For additional bonding analysis and to avoid atmospheric moisture contribution, attenuated total reflectance (ATR) technique was used (
Figs. 2 and 3). Beside IR bands observed in transmission spectra several other bands become apparent in ATR spectra. Respective identification of IR bands and estimation of their relative contribution are summarized in Table 2.

Strong carbon-related absorption bands are observed in “carbon-rich” samples: C-Hn stretching (2800cm−1) and bending (1338/1445cm−1) modes, C=C (1620cm−1) and C=O (1706cm−1) stretching modes. In all samples it is observed absorption in the spectral range 700–900cm−1 with several characteristic peaks, centered near 740cm−1, 810cm−1 and 890cm−1, that can be assigned to coupling of the Si–C, Si–O stretching and the CH3 rocking modes in the Si–(CH3)n and Si–O–(CH3)n groups [16,17]. The similar set of the IR bands in CVD a-SiOC:H films was observed and identified as vibration modes of Si–O–C related structural segments [18].

The absorption band 950–1250cm−1 (particularly in the samples with low carbon incorporation) is now obviously composed by at least four bands: 1070cm−1, 1107cm−1, 1162cm−1 and 1227cm−1. The peak at 1070cm−1 is attributed to the Si–O–Si stretching bonds in a network structure. The other peaks are assigned commonly to the ring link and the cage mode in the Si–O–Si/Si–O–C bonding structure [19–22]. Strong difference of IR-spectra in transmission mode and in ATR mode can be interpreted as evidence of structural inhomogenity in depth. ATR spectra is more sensitive to nearsurface region while FTIR transmission spectra represent absortion averaged in depth.In the present paper we do not intend to identify all of these bands in details but in frame of suggested hypothesis we can conclude that the structure of the near surface region of the films can be represented rather as polymer-like Si–O–C thin layer, in contrast to bulk of the films, which is represented mainly by inorganic SiOx bulk network (1070cm−1 band) with carbon precipitates (1620cm−1, 2800–3000cm−1) and minor Si–C bonding.

From Figs. 2 and 3 it is seen that contribution of surface polymer-like Si–O–Si/Si–O–C structure is increased with the decrease of discharge power and carbon incorporation. It can be noticed also that using SiC target decreases surface polymeric component, probably due to additional carbon flux from the target. Polymer-like nature of the surface has to be taken into account when comparing properties of the a-SiOC:H films by surface probes and bulk analysis, as well as fabricating electrical contacts.

Strong visible photoluminescence at room temperature was found in a-SiOC:H samples deposited at maximum methane/oxygen ratio (CH4/O2 ratio of flow rates of 7.1/1.4) i.e. with high carbon incorporation, regardless of sputtering target used (Si or SiC). Normalized emission and excitation spectra are presented in Fig. 4. Emission bands excited by Xe lamp at 325nm are very broad, cover all visible spectral range and are strongly distorted by the interference effect. It is seen that the PL bands of the films deposited using Si target are broader and exhibit a larger blue and ultra-violet contribution. All PL measurements were performed using 395nm edge filter. Therefore the UV part of the PL spectrum is partially cut off and probably not all high energy emission contributes to the PL. The PL of the samples deposited using SiC target, i.e. with higher carbon incorporation, exhibits an obvious red shift. The spectral position of maximum intensity in these samples can be evaluated roughly to be near 550nm (2.5eV). Excitation curves of the emission at 509nm are similar in all samples. All PLE spectra contain two peaks at 380–390nm (3.2–3.3eV) and 315–340nm (3.7–3.9eV). This suggests that the origin of PL emission is the same in all samples.

Typical PL decay curves of the carbon-rich a-SiOC:H sample at different emission wavelength is presented in Fig. 5. Decay curves are obviously not single-exponent and are well fitted by stretched-exponent, which is inherent to structural systems with broad energy distribution of electron states. By comparing decay curves for 700nm and 500nm emission it can be found that the characteristic decay time is obviously dependent on the emission energy and decreases significantly with increasing the emission energy. Strong spectral distribution of exciton life-time is probably related to the distribution of structural configuration of corresponding emission sites.

As it was evidenced by FTIR, strong PL is observed in the samples with well defined carbon segregation in the silica network. To identify structure of carbon phase we tried to use Raman scattering. Unfortunetely, luminescence background caused by laser probe makes Raman scattering measurements of carbon-rich samples very difficult. Typical Raman spectrum is represented in Fig. 6. Despite nonlinear luminescence background one can see obvious Raman band in spectra range of 1000–1600cm−1 with peaks near 1600cm−1 and 1377cm−1 that can be identified as G- and D-bands inherent to 1-st order Raman scattering spectra of amorphous carbon [23]. This observation obviously indicates the presence of amorphous carbon structural domains in the film. Thus, it is reasonable to conclude that bulk morphology of the film is represented by silicon oxide network with inclusions of amorphous carbon structural domains. In a-SiOC:H samples with low carbon concentration (K266, K265) the carbon-related Raman signal was not observed, despite no luminescent background.

Such structure interpretation makes well grounded the suggestion that most relevant analogue of light-emission species in carbon-rich a-SiOC:H film can be carbon nano-inclusions, in other words – carbon nanodots [24] dispersed in silica host. It is well known that carbon nanodots are illuminating in liquid (water) solution with emission spectra to be very broad ranging from near-UV to red [24–26]. In our case, we can suggest that carbon segregation in our material has the form of carbon nanoclusters, which are dispersed in the transparent silica host, however this needs to be checked with additional microscopic research. Such solid state thin film structures are also well suitable for evaluation of photovoltaic/optoelectronic application prospective of the material that will be done next.

Amorphous hydrogenated a-SiOC:H thin films were deposited by RF-magnetron sputtering of Si and SiC targets in Ar/CH4/O2 gas mixture at different discharge power and CH4/O2 flow rate ratio. From comparison of transmission and reflection FTIR spectra it was suggested, that the structure of the surface and the bulk of the films is quite different. The surface layer contains a significant fraction of polymer-like SiOC structural components in the form of chains and cages, while bulk of the film is represented by inorganic amorphous SiOx network with amorphous carbon precipitates. Aprovel of such interpretation needs additional experiments and will be a topic of further researches.

The samples with strong carbon precipitation provide the development of visible luminescence at room temperature. By analogy with luminescent carbon nanodots it is suggested that light emission in a-SiOC:H with carbon precipitations is related to amorphous carbon nanoclusters.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was partially supported by Ministry of Education and Science of Ukraine (Project No F2904).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Infrared and Raman Characteristic Group Frequencies
The Infra-red Spectra of Complex Molecules
